  "text": "The gRPC C++ channel creation and load balancing pipeline is implemented across multiple components in src/core/:\n\n**Channel Creation Pipeline:**\nThe entry point is ChannelCreate() in src/core/lib/surface/channel_create.cc, which creates a Channel instance (defined in src/core/lib/surface/channel.h). For client channels with the V3 stack enabled, it delegates to ClientChannel::Create() (src/core/client_channel/client_channel.h), which is the main client-side channel implementation that integrates load balancing and connectivity management.\n\n**Subchannel Allocation Logic:**\nSubchannel allocation is managed through the SubchannelPoolInterface (src/core/client_channel/subchannel_pool_interface.h) with two implementations: GlobalSubchannelPool (src/core/client_channel/global_subchannel_pool.h) for shared subchannel pooling and LocalSubchannelPool (src/core/client_channel/local_subchannel_pool.h) for per-channel pools. The Subchannel class itself is defined in src/core/client_channel/subchannel.h and implements the actual connection management to individual addresses.\n\n**Load Balancing Policies:**\nThe load balancing pipeline is built on the LoadBalancingPolicy base class (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy.h). The two fundamental policy implementations are:\n- RoundRobin: src/core/load_balancing/round_robin/round_robin.cc, which distributes requests across healthy subchannels\n- PickFirst: src/core/load_balancing/pick_first/pick_first.cc, which connects to the first available subchannel\n\nBoth policies extend from LoadBalancingPolicy and use EndpointList (src/core/load_balancing/endpoint_list.h) to manage endpoint state. Policies are registered via LoadBalancingPolicyRegistry (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy_registry.h) and created through LoadBalancingPolicyFactory (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy_factory.h). The ChildPolicyHandler (src/core/load_balancing/child_policy_handler.h) enables composable policy hierarchies.\n\n**Name Resolution Interface:**\nThe name resolution system uses the Resolver interface (src/core/resolver/resolver.h) for push/pull-based resolution. ResolverFactory (src/core/resolver/resolver_factory.h) is the abstract factory for creating resolver instances. The ResolverRegistry (src/core/resolver/resolver_registry.h) manages registered factory implementations. A concrete example is NativeClientChannelDNSResolver (src/core/resolver/dns/native/dns_resolver.cc), which implements DNS resolution using the native resolver.\n\n**Connectivity State Machine:**\nConnectivity state management is implemented through ConnectivityStateTracker (src/core/lib/transport/connectivity_state.h), which tracks channel/subchannel states (IDLE, CONNECTING, READY, TRANSIENT_FAILURE, SHUTDOWN) and notifies watchers of state transitions. The interface supports both synchronous (ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) and asynchronous (AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) notification patterns."

### **Channel Creation & Management (5 files)**

### **Load Balancing Policies (10 files)**

- **ChildPolicyHandler**: `src/core/load_balancing/child_policy_handler.h` - Composable policies

### **Connectivity State Machine (2 files)**
- **ConnectivityStateTracker**: `src/core/lib/transport/connectivity_state.h` - State tracking & notification
- Supports IDLE, CONNECTING, READY, TRANSIENT_FAILURE, and SHUTDOWN states

    38→const char* ConnectivityStateName(grpc_connectivity_state state);
    39→
    40→// Interface for watching connectivity state.
    41→// Subclasses must implement the Notify() method.
    42→//
    43→// Note: Most callers will want to use
    44→// AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface instead.
    45→class ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface
    46→    : public InternallyRefCounted<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface> {
    47→ public:
    48→  ~ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface() override = default;
    49→
    50→  // Notifies the watcher that the state has changed to new_state.
    51→  virtual void Notify(grpc_connectivity_state new_state,
    52→                      const absl::Status& status) = 0;
    53→
    54→  void Orphan() override { Unref(); }
    55→};
    56→
    57→// An alternative watcher interface that performs notifications via an
    58→// asynchronous callback scheduled on the ExecCtx.
    59→// Subclasses must implement the OnConnectivityStateChange() method.
    60→class AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface
    61→    : public ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface {
    62→ public:
    63→  ~AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface() override = default;
    64→
    65→  // Schedules a closure on the ExecCtx to invoke
    66→  // OnConnectivityStateChange() asynchronously.
    67→  void Notify(grpc_connectivity_state new_state,
    68→              const absl::Status& status) final;
    69→
    70→ protected:
    71→  class Notifier;
    72→
    73→  // If \a work_serializer is nullptr, then the notification will be scheduled
    74→  // on the ExecCtx.
    75→  explicit AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface(
    76→      std::shared_ptr<WorkSerializer> work_serializer = nullptr)
    77→      : work_serializer_(std::move(work_serializer)) {}
    78→
    79→  // Invoked asynchronously when Notify() is called.
    80→  virtual void OnConnectivityStateChange(grpc_connectivity_state new_state,
    81→                                         const absl::Status& status) = 0;
    82→
    83→ private:
    84→  std::shared_ptr<WorkSerializer> work_serializer_;
    85→};
    86→
    87→// Tracks connectivity state.  Maintains a list of watchers that are
    88→// notified whenever the state changes.
    89→//
    90→// Note that once the state becomes SHUTDOWN, watchers will be notified
    91→// and then automatically orphaned (i.e., RemoveWatcher() does not need
    92→// to be called).
    93→class ConnectivityStateTracker {
    94→ public:
    95→  explicit ConnectivityStateTracker(
    96→      const char* name, grpc_connectivity_state state = GRPC_CHANNEL_IDLE,
    97→      const absl::Status& status = absl::Status())
    98→      : name_(name), state_(state), status_(status) {}
    99→
   100→  ~ConnectivityStateTracker();
   101→
   102→  // Adds a watcher.
   103→  // If the current state is different than initial_state, the watcher
   104→  // will be notified immediately.  Otherwise, it will be notified
   105→  // whenever the state changes.
   106→  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.
   107→  void AddWatcher(grpc_connectivity_state initial_state,
   108→                  OrphanablePtr<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface> watcher);
   109→
   110→  // Removes a watcher.  The watcher will be orphaned.
   111→  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.
   112→  void RemoveWatcher(ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface* watcher);
   113→
   114→  // Sets connectivity state.
   115→  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.
   116→  void SetState(grpc_connectivity_state state, const absl::Status& status,
   117→                const char* reason);
   118→
   119→  // Gets the current state.
   120→  // Thread safe; no need to use an external lock.
   121→  grpc_connectivity_state state() const;
   122→
   123→  // Get the current status.
   124→  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.
   125→  absl::Status status() const { return status_; }
   126→
   127→  // Returns the number of watchers.
   128→  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.
   129→  size_t NumWatchers() const { return watchers_.size(); }
   130→
   131→ private:
   132→  const char* name_;
   133→  std::atomic<grpc_connectivity_state> state_{grpc_connectivity_state()};
   134→  absl::Status status_;
   135→  // TODO(roth): Once we can use C++-14 heterogeneous lookups, this can
   136→  // be a set instead of a map.
   137→  std::map<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface*,
   138→           OrphanablePtr<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface>>
   139→      watchers_;
   140→};
   141→
   142→}  // namespace grpc_core
   143→

Maintains a list of watchers that are\n// notified whenever the state changes.\n//\n// Note that once the state becomes SHUTDOWN, watchers will be notified\n// and then automatically orphaned (i.e., RemoveWatcher() does not need\n// to be called).\nclass ConnectivityStateTracker {\n public:\n  explicit ConnectivityStateTracker(\n      const char* name, grpc_connectivity_state state = GRPC_CHANNEL_IDLE,\n      const absl::Status& status = absl::Status())\n      : name_(name), state_(state), status_(status) {}\n\n  ~ConnectivityStateTracker();\n\n  // Adds a watcher.\n  // If the current state is different than initial_state, the watcher\n  // will be notified immediately.  Otherwise, it will be notified\n  // whenever the state changes.\n  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.\n  void AddWatcher(grpc_connectivity_state initial_state,\n                  OrphanablePtr<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface> watcher);\n\n  // Removes a watcher.  The watcher will be orphaned.\n  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.\n  void RemoveWatcher(ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface* watcher);\n\n  // Sets connectivity state.\n  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.\n  void SetState(grpc_connectivity_state state, const absl::Status& status,\n                const char* reason);\n\n  // Gets the current state.\n  // Thread safe; no need to use an external lock.\n  grpc_connectivity_state state() const;\n\n  // Get the current status.\n  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.\n  absl::Status status() const { return status_; }\n\n  // Returns the number of watchers.\n  // Not thread safe; access must be serialized with an external lock.\n  size_t NumWatchers() const { return watchers_.size(); }\n\n private:\n  const char* name_;\n  std::atomic<grpc_connectivity_state> state_{grpc_connectivity_state()};\n  absl::Status status_;\n  // TODO(roth): Once we can use C++-14 heterogeneous lookups, this can\n  // be a set instead of a map.\n  std::map<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface*,\n           OrphanablePtr<ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface>>\n      watchers_;\n};\n\n}  // namespace grpc_core\n\n#endif  // GRPC_SRC_CORE_LIB_TRANSPORT_CONNECTIVITY_STATE_H\n", "numLines": 145, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 145}}

    19→
    20→#include <grpc/support/port_platform.h>
    21→
    22→#include <functional>
    23→#include <string>
    24→
    25→#include "absl/status/status.h"
    26→#include "absl/status/statusor.h"

    28→#include "src/core/lib/debug/trace.h"
    29→#include "src/core/resolver/endpoint_addresses.h"
    30→#include "src/core/resolver/server_address.h"  // IWYU pragma: keep
    31→#include "src/core/service_config/service_config.h"
    32→#include "src/core/util/orphanable.h"
    33→#include "src/core/util/ref_counted_ptr.h"
    34→
    35→namespace grpc_core {
    36→
    37→/// Interface for name resolution.
    38→///
    39→/// This interface is designed to support both push-based and pull-based
    40→/// mechanisms.  A push-based mechanism is one where the resolver will
    41→/// subscribe to updates for a given name, and the name service will
    42→/// proactively send new data to the resolver whenever the data associated
    43→/// with the name changes.  A pull-based mechanism is one where the resolver
    44→/// needs to query the name service again to get updated information (e.g.,
    45→/// DNS).
    46→///
    47→/// Note: All methods with a "Locked" suffix must be called from the
    48→/// work_serializer passed to the constructor.
    49→class Resolver : public InternallyRefCounted<Resolver> {
    50→ public:
    51→  /// Results returned by the resolver.
    52→  struct Result {
    53→    /// A list of endpoints, each with one or more addresses, or an error.
    54→    absl::StatusOr<EndpointAddressesList> addresses;
    55→    /// A service config, or an error.
    56→    absl::StatusOr<RefCountedPtr<ServiceConfig>> service_config = nullptr;
    57→    /// An optional human-readable note describing context about the resolution,
    58→    /// to be passed along to the LB policy for inclusion in RPC failure status
    59→    /// messages in cases where neither \a addresses nor \a service_config
    60→    /// has a non-OK status.  For example, a resolver that returns an empty
    61→    /// address list but a valid service config may set to this to something
    62→    /// like "no DNS entries found for <name>".
    63→    std::string resolution_note;
    64→    // TODO(roth): Before making this a public API, figure out a way to
    65→    // avoid exposing channel args this way.
    66→    ChannelArgs args;
    67→    // If non-null, this callback will be invoked when the LB policy has
    68→    // processed the result.  The status value passed to the callback
    69→    // indicates whether the LB policy accepted the update.  For polling
    70→    // resolvers, if the reported status is non-OK, then the resolver
    71→    // should put itself into backoff to retry the resolution later.
    72→    // The resolver impl must not call ResultHandler::ReportResult()
    73→    // again until after this callback has been invoked.
    74→    // The callback will be invoked within the channel's WorkSerializer.
    75→    // It may or may not be invoked before ResultHandler::ReportResult()
    76→    // returns, which is why it's a separate callback.
    77→    std::function<void(absl::Status)> result_health_callback;
    78→  };
    79→
    80→  /// A proxy object used by the resolver to return results to the
    81→  /// client channel.
    82→  class ResultHandler {
    83→   public:
    84→    virtual ~ResultHandler() {}
    85→
    86→    /// Reports a result to the channel.
    87→    virtual void ReportResult(Result result) = 0;  // NOLINT
    88→  };
    89→
    90→  // Not copyable nor movable.
    91→  Resolver(const Resolver&) = delete;
    92→  Resolver& operator=(const Resolver&) = delete;
    93→  ~Resolver() override = default;
    94→
    95→  /// Starts resolving.
    96→  virtual void StartLocked() = 0;
    97→
    98→  /// Asks the resolver to obtain an updated resolver result, if
    99→  /// applicable.
   100→  ///
   101→  /// This is useful for pull-based implementations to decide when to
   102→  /// re-resolve.  However, the implementation is not required to
   103→  /// re-resolve immediately upon receiving this call; it may instead
   104→  /// elect to delay based on some configured minimum time between
   105→  /// queries, to avoid hammering the name service with queries.
   106→  ///
   107→  /// For push-based implementations, this may be a no-op.
   108→  ///
   109→  /// Note: Implementations must not invoke any method on the
   110→  /// ResultHandler from within this call.
   111→  virtual void RequestReresolutionLocked() {}
   112→
   113→  /// Resets the re-resolution backoff, if any.
   114→  /// This needs to be implemented only by pull-based implementations;
   115→  /// for push-based implementations, it will be a no-op.
   116→  virtual void ResetBackoffLocked() {}
   117→
   118→  // Note: This must be invoked while holding the work_serializer.
   119→  void Orphan() override {
   120→    ShutdownLocked();
   121→    Unref();
   122→  }
   123→
   124→ protected:
   125→  Resolver();
   126→
   127→  /// Shuts down the resolver.
   128→  virtual void ShutdownLocked() = 0;
   129→};
   130→
   131→}  // namespace grpc_core
   132→
   133→#endif  // GRPC_SRC_CORE_RESOLVER_RESOLVER_H

A push-based mechanism is one where the resolver will\n/// subscribe to updates for a given name, and the name service will\n/// proactively send new data to the resolver whenever the data associated\n/// with the name changes.  A pull-based mechanism is one where the resolver\n/// needs to query the name service again to get updated information (e.g.,\n/// DNS).\n///\n/// Note: All methods with a \"Locked\" suffix must be called from the\n/// work_serializer passed to the constructor.\nclass Resolver : public InternallyRefCounted<Resolver> {\n public:\n  /// Results returned by the resolver.\n  struct Result {\n    /// A list of endpoints, each with one or more addresses, or an error.\n    absl::StatusOr<EndpointAddressesList> addresses;\n    /// A service config, or an error.\n    absl::StatusOr<RefCountedPtr<ServiceConfig>> service_config = nullptr;\n    /// An optional human-readable note describing context about the resolution,\n    /// to be passed along to the LB policy for inclusion in RPC failure status\n    /// messages in cases where neither \\a addresses nor \\a service_config\n    /// has a non-OK status.  For example, a resolver that returns an empty\n    /// address list but a valid service config may set to this to something\n    /// like \"no DNS entries found for <name>\".\n    std::string resolution_note;\n    // TODO(roth): Before making this a public API, figure out a way to\n    // avoid exposing channel args this way.\n    ChannelArgs args;\n    // If non-null, this callback will be invoked when the LB policy has\n    // processed the result.  The status value passed to the callback\n    // indicates whether the LB policy accepted the update.  For polling\n    // resolvers, if the reported status is non-OK, then the resolver\n    // should put itself into backoff to retry the resolution later.\n    // The resolver impl must not call ResultHandler::ReportResult()\n    // again until after this callback has been invoked.\n    // The callback will be invoked within the channel's WorkSerializer.\n    // It may or may not be invoked before ResultHandler::ReportResult()\n    // returns, which is why it's a separate callback.\n    std::function<void(absl::Status)> result_health_callback;\n  };\n\n  /// A proxy object used by the resolver to return results to the\n  /// client channel.\n  class ResultHandler {\n   public:\n    virtual ~ResultHandler() {}\n\n    /// Reports a result to the channel.\n    virtual void ReportResult(Result result) = 0;  // NOLINT\n  };\n\n  // Not copyable nor movable.\n  Resolver(const Resolver&) = delete;\n  Resolver& operator=(const Resolver&) = delete;\n  ~Resolver() override = default;\n\n  /// Starts resolving.\n  virtual void StartLocked() = 0;\n\n  /// Asks the resolver to obtain an updated resolver result, if\n  /// applicable.\n  ///\n  /// This is useful for pull-based implementations to decide when to\n  /// re-resolve.  However, the implementation is not required to\n  /// re-resolve immediately upon receiving this call; it may instead\n  /// elect to delay based on some configured minimum time between\n  /// queries, to avoid hammering the name service with queries.\n  ///\n  /// For push-based implementations, this may be a no-op.\n  ///\n  /// Note: Implementations must not invoke any method on the\n  /// ResultHandler from within this call.\n  virtual void RequestReresolutionLocked() {}\n\n  /// Resets the re-resolution backoff, if any.\n  /// This needs to be implemented only by pull-based implementations;\n  /// for push-based implementations, it will be a no-op.\n  virtual void ResetBackoffLocked() {}\n\n  // Note: This must be invoked while holding the work_serializer.\n  void Orphan() override {\n    ShutdownLocked();\n    Unref();\n  }\n\n protected:\n  Resolver();\n\n  /// Shuts down the resolver.\n  virtual void ShutdownLocked() = 0;\n};\n\n}  // namespace grpc_core\n\n#endif  // GRPC_SRC_CORE_RESOLVER_RESOLVER_H\n", "numLines": 134, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 134}}

The interface supports both synchronous (ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) and asynchronous (AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) notification patterns."
}

    64→  "text": "The gRPC C++ channel creation and load balancing pipeline is implemented across multiple components in src/core/:

**Channel Creation Pipeline:**
The entry point is ChannelCreate() in src/core/lib/surface/channel_create.cc, which creates a Channel instance (defined in src/core/lib/surface/channel.h). For client channels with the V3 stack enabled, it delegates to ClientChannel::Create() (src/core/client_channel/client_channel.h), which is the main client-side channel implementation that integrates load balancing and connectivity management.

**Subchannel Allocation Logic:**
Subchannel allocation is managed through the SubchannelPoolInterface (src/core/client_channel/subchannel_pool_interface.h) with two implementations: GlobalSubchannelPool (src/core/client_channel/global_subchannel_pool.h) for shared subchannel pooling and LocalSubchannelPool (src/core/client_channel/local_subchannel_pool.h) for per-channel pools. The Subchannel class itself is defined in src/core/client_channel/subchannel.h and implements the actual connection management to individual addresses.

**Load Balancing Policies:**
The load balancing pipeline is built on the LoadBalancingPolicy base class (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy.h). The two fundamental policy implementations are:
- RoundRobin: src/core/load_balancing/round_robin/round_robin.cc, which distributes requests across healthy subchannels
- PickFirst: src/core/load_balancing/pick_first/pick_first.cc, which connects to the first available subchannel

Both policies extend from LoadBalancingPolicy and use EndpointList (src/core/load_balancing/endpoint_list.h) to manage endpoint state. Policies are registered via LoadBalancingPolicyRegistry (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy_registry.h) and created through LoadBalancingPolicyFactory (src/core/load_balancing/lb_policy_factory.h). The ChildPolicyHandler (src/core/load_balancing/child_policy_handler.h) enables composable policy hierarchies.

**Name Resolution Interface:**
The name resolution system uses the Resolver interface (src/core/resolver/resolver.h) for push/pull-based resolution. ResolverFactory (src/core/resolver/resolver_factory.h) is the abstract factory for creating resolver instances. The ResolverRegistry (src/core/resolver/resolver_registry.h) manages registered factory implementations. A concrete example is NativeClientChannelDNSResolver (src/core/resolver/dns/native/dns_resolver.cc), which implements DNS resolution using the native resolver.

**Connectivity State Machine:**
Connectivity state management is implemented through ConnectivityStateTracker (src/core/lib/transport/connectivity_state.h), which tracks channel/subchannel states (IDLE, CONNECTING, READY, TRANSIENT_FAILURE, SHUTDOWN) and notifies watchers of state transitions. The interface supports both synchronous (ConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) and asynchronous (AsyncConnectivityStateWatcherInterface) notification patterns."
